speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six. January 6, 1997.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: No problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can-- can we go back?

speaker: interviewer
text: We are rolling again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape six. January 6, 1997. [INAUDIBLE] interviewing Stan Rubens. You got the job.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got the job, and I started to work as a cook. And later on-- I spent a number of years and then later on, I became better, more proficient as a cook. I became a chef. I had for about half a year. Then I went to-- to become a chef in  a place in Surfers Paradise, in a restaurant. Got into an argument there with the owner who was putting the old food from the dining room back onto the-- to be re-served again. And that didn't sit too well with me, even though I hate throwing food away after the-- the experiences of the war. But thereafter, I-- I-- through a series of circumstances, I leased a little restaurant and I became the owner of a little restaurant. This lasted for a short time. Went back to Sydney. From Sydney, finally went to-- to Los Angeles. Because my--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight and a half years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you arrive?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was 1952. And I left in '59. I came to the-- to the United States. And friends of mine had been my sponsors that I had corresponded over the years with. Australia is a-- is a tale by itself. I could go on for hours on that but-- quite a few experiences there. But that has nothing to do really much with-- with the war, I think. Except there was an underlying drive over the years that I wanted to be able to-- be able to move from one place to another place. And so I kept on concentrating on boats in whatever spare times I had so that I could have my own boat. Eventually, I went from Australia to Los Angeles, from Los Angeles back to Holland for five years. My mother had asked me to come back. I got married in 1963. And in '66, I went back to Los Angeles. I couldn't handle-- I was close to a nervous breakdown in Holland. It was-- I felt so closed in. I felt so bad living there. The memories were so constant, grinding away at me of the things that had happened. The people continuously talking about the war. I wanted to be away from Holland so badly. It wasn't funny anymore. And finally I went back to-- to the United States in '66. And I-- I remember I stood at the airport in-- in-- in London to change planes. And I was so, so tense and so wrought up. And one of the planes started their engines, and I screamed on top of my voice. I don't know what I said, but I-- just to let go of emotion, I screamed on top of my voice into the sound of those-- those engines and to-- to let go of the frustration that I had of living in Holland and all the bad things. And never felt so happy when I finally got back to Los Angeles. Just, it was like nothing was a problem anymore. I could do anything. I could move mountains. Nothing was going to bother me. Got divorced in-- because my ex-wife didn't like it all that I had taken her away from her family.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were married to a Dutch woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married to a Dutch-- Dutch girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you marry her?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1993, in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yvonne.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: But not-- not [INAUDIBLE]. Yvonne-- Yvonne [? Wolf. ?] And so I have a lovely daughter and-- that I consider a blessing, of one of the good things from the marriage. The marriage itself was-- let's not even go into that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what is your daughter's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ronny-- same name as my sister. And they even look alike. She's like a young edition of my sister, unbelievable. And so went to-- went to Los Angeles and then made a deal with my ex-wife that I would buy a boat, and she could have a mink stole, so that she wouldn't feel that I was taking advantage of our money. I bought a little tiny boat that I got for a price that was a surprise to me because I made an offer in the hope that they were going to say no. And they said yes, and I was stuck with the little boat. Loved every minute of it. From there, went to a bigger boat and from there, I went to a bigger boat. This resulted in-- after the divorce in 1978, that I decided that I didn't want to-- my wife said that she was going to move, remarry, move to Houston, my ex-wife, that she was going to move to Houston. And one of my-- OK, because we had agreed that we would not remove each other from one another for more than 100 miles because I could see my daughter. And I said, well, OK. If you want-- I don't want to stop your happiness. If you can be happy in Houston, I guess I'll maybe I'll think of moving to Hawaii. And then if I have to fly over, the distance won't make that much difference anymore. If I have to fly from LA to Houston or from Honolulu to Houston, doesn't make that much of a difference. I have to take the plane regardless. And so she said OK. Meanwhile, I quit my job, started to put things in motion and prepared my boat, which by now had-- had increased in size over the years and was a cruising vessel, capable of doing, under power, about 1,000 miles, and of course, under sail, had unlimited power. I had already learned not-- navigation, coastal navigation. Took some private lessons in celestial navigation. Got a sextant. Checked my boat out, loaded up with three other people, water and food and sailed to Hawaii where I had a boat slip that I sailed in directly from-- my Newport Beach boat slip into the boat slip into-- And the reason why I wanted to do that, into Kaneohe, the reason why he wanted to do that, because I had the need to prove to myself--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: The reason was--

speaker: interviewee
text: The reason was so that I had to prove to myself that I had a capability to-- to move myself from any part in this world to any other part in the world. And if you consider that the stretch from Los Angeles to Honolulu is one of the longest stretches of nowhere any land in the world, being able to navigate myself right on the button-- within an hour's time I predicted my arrival or the sight of land-- gave me a feeling of self-confidence, knowing that I-- I could literally go-- if an emergency would take place, a war would break out, I would say, hey, to hell with it. I'm getting out of this place. I'll go where there is no war or where I will be safe. I didn't want to be caught in any war ever again. So that's why I got so involved into-- into boating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a-- are there any other way that the war influenced your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the influences are-- are so manyfold-- food. First of all, the thought of running out of food is always in the back of my mind. Feeling secure, another point. Three, weather-- I don't like cold weather because I had so much coldness in my life. I don't-- the thought of being exposed to freezing temperatures is-- is-- is just devastating to me. Witness the fact that I'm now living in-- in one of the warmest climates in the world, in Honolulu. As a matter of fact, it's interesting, if you look at the psychology of this, that I could not be further away from Holland than where I am at the moment. Regardless which direction-- east, west-- I would travel, I would travel back to Europe or Holland. I couldn't-- I'm 12 hours away or daylight savings time, 11 hours. So this is the furthest point from Holland. All I wanted to do was go west, go west, go west. Go west, young man, right, as the Americans say.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- yeah, cold weather, also, being prepared. I represent as an-- as an agent, as a rep, I have like maybe 20 different companies that I represent. So if any one of those company says, no, we don't need you any longer, I have all kinds of other companies to fall back upon so that I will not-- I will-- I have created a certain amount of security for myself that I-- I can cope with a situation one way or another. If that doesn't work, then that will work. If that doesn't work, then that doesn't work.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another thing is-- this is totally ridiculous-- but I have, outside here, I have a balcony, a lanai. And in Holland, we have-- if you want to hoist up a piano, you have those hoists on the outside of the-- of the building because the-- the stairways are too small. And this-- this place has two entrances and exits. That's how I put it together. If ever the thought would occur-- suppose the Russians would want to invade-- invade here, want to track me down. I can get out through the lower entrance, and I have a pulley and tackle situation hanging over on at the side of the lanai, balcony, so that I can, in an emergency, could skinny down there and get out through a side way. Anyhow, there's ways to get away. I've nobody-- I've told nobody actually about this, that I have that pulley and rope hanging there for that reason just as an emergency exit. But even in case of fire, I have a way to get out.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there's all kinds of thinking processes that I got to watch our for this, for that, for that, for that, for that. You-- you-- your life-- life is not the same anymore after a war. Your thinking process changes. There are circumstances that could develop that you cannot control. You want to be able to remain in control of the circumstances. I would love to have a gun, but I'm afraid to have a gun in the house. I'm afraid that it might be used against me. So that's why I don't have a gun. How do you feel about being Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a time when if people asked me if I was Jewish, I was denying it. I-- I know many people that if you ask them-- that I know that are Jewish-- if you would ask them, they will flatly deny that they are Jewish out of fear that people might know. The result is that I was, for a while, that way. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I once was stopped at a-- at a gas station and a-- a Mexican fellow says to me-- was taking my credit card. Oh, he said, Rubens, you're Jewish, huh? I said, no. I said, I'm not Jewish. I'm from-- I'm from Belgium. I'm Catholic. Rubens is a Catholic name because Peter Paul Rubens was Catholic. And he said, oh, oh. And I denied it. And yet, I did not want to deny it. So on the one hand, I deny it, on the other--

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, what-- what happened over the years, I-- some time ago, about eight, 10 years ago, I was technical advisor on the play, that a friend of mine put together. Who asked me if I would play Mr. Frank, which are flatly denied. And somehow a cleansing or an-- came out of this by being so involved in this play that if now somebody asks me if I am Jewish, yes, I am. And I'm not afraid to talk about it anymore. The result of-- of being so involved with this play is that I can very objectively talk about my wartime experiences. Occasionally, I-- it's tough, but I-- because of the experiences and being so involved in this play-- which in the beginning was like a horror story-- a horror story for me-- but I went through so many rehearsals and play-- watching the play that now it has cleansed me from my experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: And if anybody would ever-- my sister cannot talk about the war. I once asked her some questions. I wanted to write a book about-- about what had happened to me. And I asked her some questions about years. Do you remember when this and this happened? And she looked at me, and she said, well, I said, do you remember when this and this happened? And she looked at me, and-- like horror struck and broke out in hysterical crying. She said, I don't want to talk about it. Why are you talking about it? I don't want to talk about it. Could not talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I talked the other day. I talked to somebody and she said, no, I don't want to get involved. I don't want to talk about it. And as I said, if it wouldn't be for the fact that I was involved in the Anne Frank Diary play as a technical advisor and sound advisor-- because this has like the largest number of sound effects of any play in it. If I had not been involved in that, I don't think I could sit here and discuss with you all the things that happened in the manner that I am discussing this, in an almost objective fashion. It-- it totally cleared my mind of the bad experience. I've learned to accept it. I've learned to live with it. And it-- it enabled me to say goodbye to my boat without having the fear of not having to go-- not knowing I couldn't go somewhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how do you personally feel about being a Jew?

speaker: interviewee
text: It depends. Jewishness can be defined in so many different ways. You have the--

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your way?

speaker: interviewee
text: You have the Orthodox view, and I never forget in a-- in a convention, my boss in the camera business was very strict Jewish, kosher. And there were the people from New York, walking in-- walking the aisles and they had high hat, the black coats, the white shirt, and the payots. And I said to him, I said, I just can't believe how these people can be dressed like this. And he looked at me and said, what do you mean? I said, I mean-- I mean, it's abnormal, the way they are dressed. And he looked me straight in the eyes, said, they don't think so. They think that you are abnormally dressed. And I said to him, you're right.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's all a question of relativity. Where is your mindset? My mindset is that I-- for me to live as a typical Jew would be a horror story for me. Because it would bring back too much of the experiences of the war, too close for comfort. I can live with the idea of being Jewish as long as I'm not being pinned down by it and as long as it's not being marked, I don't feel marked by it. So I'm very liberal. I-- I don't go to the synagogue anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a friend here who, on a regular basis, tries to drag me over for Yom Kippur to the synagogue. And I have been a couple of times. And one day I said to the rabbi, I said, tell me, I said, you are repeating over and over again the same prayer. I-- I cannot comprehend the need for you to continuously repeat the same over and over again. What is the significance of your repeating Adonai, Adonai, Adonai, Adonai, Adonai, Eloheinu, Eloheinu, and all that stuff more. And he said-- well, he said, it's an-- an emphasizing, an admonition that you are what you are and you are reconfirming, you're-- there's one word I'm looking for-- you're reconfirming yourself to the Lord. I said, I have no need to reconfirm myself. I know where I am. I know where I am standing. For me to reconfirm myself is-- is overkill. I am Jewish. I admit to that. I will not deny it. I do not need another person to emphasize the fact this any further than where I am standing. I will act as a Christian person or as a Jewish person and I will always try and do as much good to another people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I will not cheat. I will not treat people in an unfair fashion. And I can guarantee you that I don't have to look over my shoulder because all my dealings over all the years. And I can sleep at night like a rose because I haven't done anybody any bad. What more do I have to do as a good human, as a humane person, as a mensch, right? So the Jewishness, that is for me there. I accept it. I do not pursue it. I get sometimes religious advertisement. I toss them in the garbage can. I don't want to be involved. I have to clarify one thing. You mentioned that you-- right in between, this-- before you came to Australia-- you said something. That's when my name became Stan. Did you have a different name at birth? Oh yeah. My-- my-- my name-- I was named after two grandfathers and they-- their names are Solomon. And so they named me [? Solow, ?] for short for Solomon. And I was on the which was a French crew and a lot of French people on board. And I introduced myself to the first person. I said, hello, my name is [? Solow. ?] And the guy burst out laughing. And I said, well, what's so funny? He says, well don't you know what a [? solow ?] is? I said, no, I don't. He said, well, [? solow ?] in French is dirty, rotten, son of a bitch. You just called yourself a dirty, rotten, son of a bitch. And I said, aye, aye, aye. So I-- I went around for a while, trying to figure out what kind of a name I'll use, and I came up with the name Stan, as close as possible to [? Solow ?] that had some of the same ring. And then I reconfirmed that in-- in Australia and I everybody agreed, yes, I should call myself Stan instead of [? Solow, ?] which has a very strange connotation and depends on which language you use, of course. And eventually I had my name officially changed in Holland, when I went back to Holland. In [INAUDIBLE] I had by the-- lawfully, had my name changed from Solomon to Stan Rubens. And so that's what my passport reads at the moment. I have a last question for you. Have-- do you have something to give to the people who are going to watch this video later on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I-- I have by-- by the end of next month, I will have published about 36 of my own songs and I have two of my music albums out. And perhaps it would be interesting that if I did one of my songs, which, in a way, is a subconscious reflection of the terrible days of the war. And if you listen to the words, you most likely will find certain, almost to a certain religious point, to-- the influence of what the war did and though I'm hedging-- not directly. It doesn't really talk about the war, but the meaning of the song is extremely deep. And it's called 'Strange Day Today,' and it-- it covers the feeling of a dream. And I guess the dream has to do still with the nightmares that I had. So many-- many of the songs I've written out of my subconscious mind, I guess, because I'm very, very privileged, very lucky. I can write something in 15 minutes time. I have drafted a song. And this 'Strange Day,' I don't think I spent more than 15 minutes in originally drafting it. Completing it and doing the recordings-- it is on tape already-- took me quite a bit longer. But over the years, I have gotten quite proficient at that, so I'm releasing my-- my third album in the next few months. And my music, I'll keep on going. By the way, I switched from guitar to electronic keyboard because the guitar did not have the expansion possibilities of what I had in my mind. When I say what I have in my mind, I am not sure if I mentioned this, but I will turn off the radio because I have continuously music going through my mind. And the radio becomes an interference to my music that is going through my mind.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to listen to your song later on.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I will thank you for sharing your story with us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I feel privileged and grateful that I-- I can be here and talk about it and give you an insight what it was like in those days and how we survived. And it-- it's only-- the one thing that I told the people of-- the people of-- that did the play, Anne Frank Diary, there's just no way I can explain the incredible amount of fear that was surrounding us all the time of what is going to happen next. How are we going to get through this day?

speaker: interviewee
text: What is hanging over our head? Can we survive? Fear, discomfort, cold, and hunger were the-- were the things that-- almost in Dickensonian-like style were like a cloud around us all the time. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just about. All right. We're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, this is a photograph of my grandmother and grandfather from my father's side, the Rubens family. My grandmother on the left, obviously, my grandfather on the right. I never met him. He passed away before I was born. So this photograph dates back to 1935 or '36, approximately.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what are the names?

speaker: interviewee
text: The names are-- his name, my-- my grandfather's name was Hartog and my you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: His first was Hartog?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hartog, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And his last name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-A-R-T-O-G You know, I don't remember the name of my grandmother. She was just Oma and that was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oma means grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was Hartog Rubens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hartog Rubens, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was his profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe he was a butcher. [LAUGHS] I'm not even sure what he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a kosher butcher?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't remember. After-- after the war, my father never talked about him. My mother never talked about him. It was never discussed, what he did. But I believe he was a butcher.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. This is a photograph of my mother, who was often compared with Queen Elizabeth in her days. I think she, at this point, she is about 40 years old or 38.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you mentioned her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Eva, but she was always called Evelyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Last name, of course, is Rubens. Themans, her maiden name is Themans-- T-H-E-M-A-N-S. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was married when she was 18 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I guess I'm looking at a picture of Stan Rubens when he was about seven or eight years old, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when-- where this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think this is a school picture, taken at school. And oh, that's all I have to say on that one, not much, really. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I needed new glasses, quite obviously, because these glasses are getting too small for my face.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the identification papers of my dad, showing our address and where he was born. It has the signature, his fingerprints on it. And on the other side, it has his photograph and it will show also a J on it to indicate that he is Jewish or as the German language would say, Jude. We have to turn it over now in a moment. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's actually a historical document because there's-- there's not many of these kinds of documents around.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, rolling.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other side of the document here shows clearly the two Js, not to be mistaken for Jewish, Jew. A photograph of my father, identification. I've only known him as being bald-headed, so-- this is a photo of my dad. These-- This is a rough sketch of the hiding place on [INAUDIBLE], where we were hiding. This indicates windows here. This is the stairway going up and then this is the hallway. This is a continuation of the stairway going up further. There was a bedroom here. The kitchen was here. This is the bedroom I was sleeping in, in a little-- against this wall here. You see the dotted line. Originally, the dotted line indicates where the two sliding doors were, because it was what I call room en-suite. The rooms were adjoined by the sliding doors. This also later on changed by creating a panel. This entire panel inside this wardrobe here was removable from top to bottom and then you could walk into this hiding place here, which was about three and half feet wide and the length of the room, to this wardrobe, that is. And the cash box was hidden right over-- excuse me-- was hidden-- was hidden right over here. And I-- I used to spend time in that hiding place from time to time and relax and be away from everybody. And this is the living room, and we would look from these windows and could look outside. That's about it on this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the area in front the schouwburg, the theater, where my parents were being kept here. This is the creche or the children's area here. We would then walk across. This is the safe haven for people to stand, waiting for the tram. And this is the other side. So these are the-- the trail-- the-- the tracks of the tram here. I walked with the-- my-- my person that was going to take me across to here. I walked over like this. And when I got to here, I took a bee line over to here, to the side street. And approximately here is where I wasn't able to continue my--
